---
title: "Lab 02 - Introduction to `dplyr` - INSTRUCTIONS"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set( echo = TRUE )
```

# Overview

In this lab, you will begin learning how to manipulate and summarize data using the `dplyr` package in R. 
The lab is divided into two parts. 
First, you will work through a guided example using DATA to practice the core `dplyr` workflow. 
After completing the guided exercises, you will apply the same techniques to the dataset you selected in Lab 1.

The purpose of this lab is to develop familiarity with the “grammar” of data manipulation and build confidence using reproducible workflows to prepare data for analysis.

# Part I

## Load and Inspect the Dataset

Import the dataset into R and examine its structure using functions such as: `head()`, `str()`, `glimpse()`, `summary()`. 
Write a brief description of the dataset, including:

  - Number of rows and columns
  - Important variables
  - Any obvious data quality concerns

## Practice Core `dplyr` Verbs

Use the following functions:

HERE NEED TO THINK THROUGH WHAT YOU WOULD ACTUALLY WANT THEM TO 
DO HERE; THIS WILL BE LINKED TO THE DATA YOU PROVIDE

HERE NEED TO THINK THROUGH WHAT YOU WOULD ACTUALLY WANT THEM TO 
DO HERE; THIS WILL BE LINKED TO THE DATA YOU PROVIDE


HERE NEED TO THINK THROUGH WHAT YOU WOULD ACTUALLY WANT THEM TO 
DO HERE; THIS WILL BE LINKED TO THE DATA YOU PROVIDE


HERE NEED TO THINK THROUGH WHAT YOU WOULD ACTUALLY WANT THEM TO 
DO HERE; THIS WILL BE LINKED TO THE DATA YOU PROVIDE


HERE NEED TO THINK THROUGH WHAT YOU WOULD ACTUALLY WANT THEM TO 
DO HERE; THIS WILL BE LINKED TO THE DATA YOU PROVIDE




select()
filter()
arrange()
mutate()
group_by()
summarize()

Your work should demonstrate how each function changes or summarizes the dataset.

3. Create Summary Tables

Produce at least two summary tables using grouped statistics or counts.

Examples include:

Counts by category
Average values by group
Top or bottom observations
Totals or percentages
Part 2: Apply the Workflow to Your Dataset
Using Your Dataset from Lab 1

Now apply the same workflow to the dataset you selected in Lab 1.

Tasks
1. Load and Explore Your Dataset

Import your dataset into R and briefly describe:

The structure of the dataset
Key variables
Any missing or inconsistent values you notice
2. Apply Basic dplyr Functions

Use at least four of the following functions on your dataset:

select()
filter()
arrange()
mutate()
group_by()
summarize()

You should demonstrate meaningful transformations relevant to your dataset.

3. Produce Summary Output

Create at least one summary table that helps describe patterns or trends in your data.

RMarkdown Report

Document your work in an RMarkdown report that includes:

Section headers for Part 1 and Part 2
Code chunks showing your work
Brief written explanations of what each step accomplishes
Summary tables and outputs
A short reflection describing what you learned about your dataset

The report should include both code and results.

Deliverables

Submit the following:

Your completed RMarkdown (.Rmd) file
A rendered version of the report (.html, .pdf, or .docx)
Any modified dataset files you created during the lab
Looking Ahead

The skills practiced in this lab will be expanded in future weeks as students learn to:

Reshape and combine datasets
Create visualizations with ggplot2
Build interactive dashboards with Shiny
Develop reproducible analytical workflows using RStudio and the Tidyverse


HERE IS THE PIECE ABOUT A VIDEO THEY NEED TO DO

Record a Short Walkthrough Video

Create a short video presentation (approximately 3–5 minutes) in which you walk through your dataset and explain the work you completed in Part 2.

Your video should include:

A brief introduction to your dataset
An explanation of the variables you selected for analysis
A demonstration of at least two dplyr functions you used
A discussion of one summary table or result you produced
A brief reflection on any challenges or interesting findings from the dataset

You may record your screen using Zoom, Panopto, OBS, or another screen-recording tool. The video should clearly show your RStudio workflow and include narration explaining your analytical process.

Submit the video link or upload according to course instructions.











# How to Submit

Download the [template for this lab](../labs/templates/lab-02-LastName.zip?raw=true) prior to beginning the lab. 

<br>

### Knitting to HTML

When you have completed your assignment, click the "Knit" button to render your `.RMD` file into a `.HTML` report.

<br>

### Special Instructions

Upload both your `.RMD` and `.HTML` files to the appropriate link for this assignment on the Canvas page for this course.

<br>

### Before You Submit

Remember to ensure the following before submitting your assignment.

  * Name your files using this format: **Lab-##-LastName.rmd** and **Lab-##-LastName.html**
  * Show both the solution for your code and write out your answers in the body text 

See [Google's R Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/Rguide.xml) for examples of common conventions.

<br>
<br>

### Common Knitting Issues

`.RMD` files are knit into `.HTML` and other formats procedural, or line-by-line.

* An error in code when knitting will halt the process; error messages will tell you the specific line with the error
* Certain functions, like `install.packages()` or `setwd()`, are bound to cause errors in knitting 
* Altering a dataset or variable in one chunk will affect its use in all later chunks
* If an object is "not found", make sure it was created or loaded with `library()` in a previous chunk

**If All Else Fails:** If you cannot determine and fix the errors in a code chunk that's preventing you from knitting your document, add `eval = FALSE` inside the brackets of `{r}` at the beginning of a chunk to ensure that R does not attempt to evaluate it, that is: `{r eval = FALSE}`. This will prevent an erroneous chunk of code from halting the knitting process.  

<br>

###### ***Please*** report any needed corrections to the [Issues](https://github.com/Watts-College/crj-507-spring-2026/issues/new) page. Thanks!
